_disclaimer: SPECULATIVE discretionary research, NOT a backtested edge. Live snapshot, not point-in-time. Individual-stock survivorship applies.

ideas.thesis: Intuit fell ~67% from its 52-week high to near a multi-year low ($267 vs $813 high), yet it still beat Q3 FY26 EPS ($12.80 vs $12.57, +1.8%), carries 33 buys vs 4 sells, a $452.94 consensus target (~70% implied upside), 14.9% ROE and rising forward EPS. The bull case is a classic overshoot in a high-quality compounder (QuickBooks ecosystem + Credit Karma) trading at a depressed ~13x forward EPS. The crucial caveat: the sell-off is NOT pure sentiment - the May earnings call confirmed the TurboTax DIY/Consumer business is losing share at the low end with the DIY tax segment estimated declining ~14-15%, and management was directly pressed on GenAI changing the competitive landscape. That is a partial structural threat, not a clean overreaction.
ideas.key_risk: The Consumer/TurboTax decline is at least partly secular: free GenAI tax/accounting agents could permanently compress the DIY funnel (a 2M-unit drop in free filers was flagged), and consensus targets may lag if estimates are cut further. This is a value-with-a-falling-knife setup, not a clean V-bottom.

news.headline: Intuit Q3 2026 Earnings Call - analysts press on TurboTax share loss at low end and GenAI competitive threat ('2023/2024 deja vu')
Intuit Conference (Jun 2026) - DIY tax business modeled to decline ~14-15%; questions on stabilizing churn
10-Q strategic risk disclosure flags competitors deploying AI that 'may reduce customer demand'

competitors.name: Salesforce, Inc.
competitors.note: Larger software peer also crushed (~42% YTD); shares same AI-disruption-of-incumbent-SaaS narrative; not a direct product rival but a valuation read

competitors.name: Adobe Inc.
competitors.note: Another premium software franchise re-rated to single-digit P/E on identical GenAI-incumbent fears - shows INTU's de-rating is sector-wide, not idiosyncratic

ideas.thesis: ServiceNow is down ~55% from its high to $95 (near the bottom of its 52-week range) on a valuation reset and fear that AI labs will commoditize enterprise software - yet the underlying business is the strongest in this basket: 58 buys vs 1 sell, $150.26 consensus (~58% implied upside), ~28% operating margin, ~16% ROE, and forward EPS rising steadily through 2029. The bull case is that the decline is a multiple de-rating of an entrenched, mission-critical platform rather than a fundamentals break: an independent analyst (Jun 2026) explicitly named ServiceNow a 'winner' that is 'impossible to replace' and an AI tailwind, while the company is shipping Now Assist AI across all SKUs with simplified pricing. The drawdown looks more like sentiment/froth-unwind than justified structural decay.
ideas.key_risk: The Q1 in-line print (EPS $0.97 vs $0.97, 0% surprise) and the post-print 12% drop show the market wants AI-driven organic acceleration NOW - if Now Assist ARR stays small versus the AI labs' net-new ARR, the 'classic stack gets negotiated down to fund AI' dynamic could cap growth and the de-rating could persist longer.

news.headline: Analyst names ServiceNow a clear AI winner: 'impossible to replace', growth can accelerate via upsell + AI
Q1 2026 call: stock down 12% after-hours on lack of organic AI acceleration vs AI labs adding $5B net-new ARR/qtr
DEF 14A notes NOW fell 12-16% YTD in 2025 on a sub-consensus subscription forecast and premium-multiple 'risk-off'

competitors.name: Salesforce, Inc.
competitors.note: Direct enterprise-app rival, cheaper P/E but slower growth and weaker margin; also down ~42% YTD - shared narrative

competitors.name: Workday, Inc.
competitors.note: HCM/financials peer down ~46% YTD; comparable growth but NOW has higher margin and stronger competitive moat ('one data model')

ideas.thesis: Adobe is down ~50% from its high to $195 (just above its 52-week low) and trades at roughly 7-8x forward EPS - an extraordinarily compressed multiple for a franchise still beating estimates (Q2 FY26 EPS $5.96 vs $5.82, +2.4%; revenue beat 2.5%), growing ~11%, with ~25% margins and rising forward EPS. The bull case is a deep-value, heavy-FCF moat (Creative Cloud + Acrobat + Experience Cloud) that could spring-load on any sentiment turn. But this is genuinely mixed: the latest leg down was self-inflicted/structural-leaning - Adobe deliberately cut its FY26 organic ARR outlook by ~$500M and dropped its organic ARR growth target, deferring Creative Cloud price increases to chase freemium AI users (organic ARR growth now ~8%), and lost BOTH its CEO (transition announced) and CFO (Dan Durn to Marvell). Multiple firms downgraded post-earnings (Evercore to In Line PT $225; Stifel; Jefferies/Boursorama Hold PT $230).
ideas.key_risk: AI monetization is unanswered and the leadership vacuum (new CEO + interim CFO) means sentiment may not turn until both seats are filled, per Evercore. The freemium pivot is a real revenue-growth haircut, not just optics - if generative-AI rivals (OpenAI, Google, Canva, Midjourney) keep pressuring the funnel, the 'value trap' case is live.

news.headline: Evercore ISI downgrades Adobe to In Line, PT cut to $225 from $325, after ~$500M FY26 ARR reset; sees no sentiment turn until new CEO/CFO in place
Adobe's strong quarter overshadowed by CFO Dan Durn exit (to Marvell) and growth uncertainty
Adobe poised to outperform peers on AI growth and Cloud synergies (RBC), hosting fireside on GenAI monetization

competitors.name: Salesforce, Inc.
competitors.note: Adjacent enterprise-software peer; trades higher P/E than ADBE despite slower growth - ADBE optically cheaper but carries the freemium/ARR-reset overhang

competitors.name: ServiceNow, Inc.
competitors.note: Higher-growth, higher-margin SaaS peer commands ~3x ADBE's multiple - the market is paying for growth durability ADBE just walked back

ideas.thesis: Nike is down ~44% from its high to $45 near a multi-year low, but the setup is a self-inflicted, management-driven turnaround rather than a secular collapse - and the most recent print is the strongest positive surprise in the basket (Q3 FY26 EPS $0.35 vs $0.29 estimated, +20.4%). CEO Elliott Hill is executing a deliberate reset: pulling back classic-franchise supply, repositioning Nike Digital as full-price, liquidating inventory via markdowns (the cause of the margin trough), and reinvesting in wholesale and demand creation. North America has made the most progress; gross margin already troughed (Q1 FY26 down 320bps to 42.2% on clearance + tariffs) and forward EPS shows a clear FY27 recovery shape. With 37 buys vs 5 sells, a $62 consensus (~37% upside) and the strongest brand in apparel, this looks like a recovery off a managed trough, not a broken business.
ideas.key_risk: The turnaround is back-half-weighted and geographically uneven: Greater China (declining store traffic, heavy promotion, channel inventory) and Converse remain drags expected to persist into FY27, and trough ROE (~3.7%) plus tariff cost pressure mean a slower-than-hoped margin recovery would re-rate the stock down. It is a multi-quarter execution bet on Hill's reset taking hold.

news.headline: Nike Q3 FY26: EPS $0.35 vs $0.29 est (+20% surprise); revenue beat; CFO says shifts position portfolio 'for a full recovery'
10-Q: turnaround actions to complete by end-2026; North America furthest along, Greater China + Converse drag into FY27
Q1 FY26: gross margin -320bps to 42.2% on wholesale/factory markdowns, tariffs and channel mix (inventory liquidation underway)

competitors.name: Deckers Outdoor Corporation
competitors.note: Hoka/UGG owner is the share-taker eating into Nike's running franchise - DECK is UP ~7% over 1Y and trades mid-range (pos 63%), the opposite tape of NKE; rival has momentum

competitors.name: Lululemon Athletica Inc.
competitors.note: Also crushed (~51% off high, near 52wk low) - shows the beaten-down apparel trade is broad; LULU growth has slowed, so NKE's brand scale + turnaround optionality stands out

ideas.thesis: Comcast sits at essentially a multi-year low ($22.43, 0.4% of its 52-week range) and screens optically cheap (~6x forward EPS, 40% implied upside to a $31.48 consensus, beat Q1 by 9%). But this is the clearest falling-knife in the basket and the decline is justified/structural, not an overreaction: the core broadband business is in secular decline (181k subs lost in Q4 - a steeper drop YoY; full-year 2026 loss guided ~590k), ARPU is now FALLING (-3.1%), and the company is buying retention with free-wireless-line giveaways and a 5-year price guarantee that further pressures ARPU and EBITDA (Connectivity EBITDA -4% YoY). New existential threats are emerging (Rosenblatt cut to $24 citing Starlink/SpaceX broadband competition; fixed-wireless from T-Mobile). Forward EPS is essentially flat-to-down, not rising. A shareholder at the June AGM called it a company that 'requires repositioning' - the $30B NBCU networks now valued ~$5B post-Versant spinoff, and Comcast lost both the Warner Bros and the bid contests.
ideas.key_risk: If broadband losses do not stabilize and Starlink/fixed-wireless accelerate, the cheap multiple is a value trap and 'cheap gets cheaper'; the 4.5%+ dividend is the main support, but a low-growth cable utility in secular share loss does not fit a pre-explosion breakout fingerprint. This is a yield/defensive name, not a turnaround spring.

news.headline: Comcast broadband losses deepen as competition intensifies; 181k subs lost in Q4, ARPU only +1%, Connectivity EBITDA -4%
Comcast PT lowered to $24 at Rosenblatt; new investor concern is Starlink broadband competition ahead of SpaceX IPO
AGM: shareholder calls Comcast a company needing 'repositioning'; $30B NBCU cable nets (Versant) now ~$5B, lost WBD bid

competitors.name: Charter Communications, Inc.
competitors.note: Fellow cable operator in worse shape - down ~67% over 1Y, at its 52wk low, net-debt/EBITDA ~18-22x; confirms cable is a structurally declining sector, not a single-name dip

competitors.name: T-Mobile US, Inc.
competitors.note: The share-taker: T-Mobile's 5G fixed-wireless is a primary cause of Comcast's broadband losses; growing, profitable, far shallower drawdown - the rival eating Comcast's lunch
